metadata.summary: Build a YouTube channel with niche positioning, searchable formats, hook-first structure, and sustainable cadence. Optimize search-intent videos first (tutorials, how-to) before discovery content.

content.title: YouTube Strategy
content.summary: A content-discovery loop for building a YouTube channel around a product or personal brand: niche positioning, searchable video formats, hook-first structure, thumbnail + title optimization, and a sustainable publishing cadence. The core rule: optimize for search intent videos first (tutorials, how-to) before attempting discovery content (opinion, commentary).

why.p: YouTube is the second-largest search engine and the highest-trust platform for demonstrating expertise. Early focus on searchable, long-form tutorials compounds — each video generates traffic for years. Most channels fail because they prioritize production quality over consistency and search intent.

list.item: Building a content marketing channel around a technical or educational product.
Generating search-driven top-of-funnel awareness where blog SEO is too competitive.
Repurposing existing written content (blog posts, docs) into video format.
Adding a YouTube component to an existing content distribution stack.
Brand authority building for a solopreneur or technical founder.
No defined niche or ICP — generalist channels rarely grow; algorithm rewards niche focus.
No commitment to at least 2 videos/month for 6+ months — YouTube is compounding, not quick.
Audience is not on YouTube (e.g., B2B enterprise compliance buyers).
Production bottleneck is unresolved — inconsistency kills momentum faster than poor quality.

section.name: niche-and-formats
section.p: Niche formula, validation criteria, content pillars, and video format selection by goal.

rule.statement: Define the channel niche as: [Your expertise] + [Specific audience] + [Content format]. Validate all three axes before publishing.
rule.rationale: YouTube's algorithm rewards consistent niche signals. Generalist channels struggle to build a loyal subscriber base because the algorithm cannot identify who to recommend the channel to.

example.title: Niche definition example
example.note: SaaS marketing + indie founders + tutorials and case studies. Specific expertise, identifiable audience, consistent format.

example.title: Generalist niche
example.note: Marketing tips for everyone. No expertise specificity, no audience definition, no format constraint — the algorithm has no signal to optimize.

rule.statement: Validate a niche on four dimensions before committing: demand, differentiation, sustainability (100+ video ideas), and monetization path.
rule.rationale: A niche with demand but no monetization path produces views without revenue. A niche with a product but no demand produces content nobody watches.

list.item: Demand: are people actively searching for this on YouTube?
Competition: can you differentiate from existing channels in this niche?
Sustainability: can you generate 100+ video ideas without forcing it?
Monetization: is there a product, service, or course to sell to this audience?

rule.statement: Choose 3-4 content pillars and map every video to at least one pillar. Do not publish videos outside the pillars.
rule.rationale: Pillar discipline maintains the algorithm signal and subscriber expectations. Off-pillar videos fragment the audience and reduce subscriber conversion rate.

list.item: Educational: how-to, tutorials, explainers.
Entertainment: stories, commentary, opinion.
Inspiration: case studies, interviews, build-in-public.
Updates: news, trends, product announcements.

rule.statement: Prioritize search-intent formats (tutorials, list videos) during the first 6-12 months. Switch to discovery formats (opinion, commentary) only after establishing a subscriber base.
rule.rationale: Search-intent videos rank for queries indefinitely, compounding traffic. Discovery content depends on algorithmic push, which requires watch-time history to earn.

list.item: Tutorials (10-20 min): search traffic, authority building.
List videos (8-15 min): easy to produce, highly searchable.
Case studies (15-30 min): trust building, conversion.
Interviews (30-60 min): audience borrowing, relationship building.
Shorts (<60 sec): top-of-funnel discovery, supplement long-form.

section.name: video-structure-and-optimization
section.p: Hook-first video structure, title and thumbnail rules, and description template.

rule.statement: Write the hook before the rest of the script. The first 30 seconds determine whether viewers stay; a strong hook doubles effective watch time.
rule.rationale: YouTube's algorithm weights watch time and session time. A video with a poor hook that loses viewers in the first 30 seconds generates negative distribution signals regardless of total content quality.

list.item: 0:00-0:30 — Hook: grab attention, preview value (one of: question, promise, curiosity, contrarian).
0:30-1:00 — Intro: who you are, credibility signal, agenda.
1:00-near end — Content: value delivery with chapter markers.
Near end — CTA: subscribe, watch next video, product link (30 sec max).

example.title: Question hook
example.note: Question hook: "Ever wondered why your YouTube videos get views but zero subscribers?" — creates tension, promises answer, audience self-selects.

rule.statement: Structure every title as: [Primary keyword] + [Benefit or outcome] + [Optional curiosity element]. Keep under 60 characters.
rule.rationale: Title and thumbnail must be designed together as a single unit — the combination drives CTR. Keyword in the title signals search relevance; benefit + curiosity drives the click.

example.title: Title formula
example.note: "How to Get Your First 1000 Subscribers (What Actually Works)" — keyword first, outcome stated, curiosity parenthetical.

example.title: Poor title
example.note: "My YouTube Journey Update #47" — no keyword, no benefit, no audience signal. Only works for established channels with loyal subscribers.

rule.statement: Every thumbnail must be readable at 120x68 pixels (mobile thumbnail size) before publishing.
rule.rationale: Most YouTube impressions appear at small thumbnail sizes on mobile. A thumbnail that only reads at large size is effectively invisible to the majority of the audience.

list.item: Face with expression (surprise, excitement) — highest CTR signal.
3-4 words maximum on the thumbnail.
Contrasting colors against YouTube's white/dark background.
Consistent style and branding across all thumbnails.

rule.statement: Add timestamp chapters to all videos above 8 minutes. YouTube surfaces individual chapters in search results, increasing impressions per video.
rule.rationale: Chapter timestamps generate additional SERP appearances for each chapter heading, multiplying the indexable surface area of a single video.

section.reference: YouTube description template with keyword hook, timestamps, resources, tags

section.name: metrics-and-publishing
section.p: Performance metric targets, publishing cadence rules, and common mistakes that kill channel growth.

rule.statement: Track CTR, average view duration percentage, and subscriber conversion rate as primary health signals — not absolute view counts.
rule.rationale: Absolute view counts are a lagging indicator and depend on subscriber count. CTR and watch duration are leading indicators that predict algorithmic distribution.

list.item: CTR: 4% good, 8%+ great (below 4% → fix thumbnail or title).
Average view duration: 40% good, 60%+ great (below 40% → fix hook or pacing).
Subscriber conversion: 3% good, 8%+ great (below 3% → add subscribe CTA or improve value).

rule.statement: Choose a publishing cadence you can sustain for 12 months. A consistent weekly schedule outperforms an inconsistent aggressive schedule.
rule.rationale: YouTube's algorithm uses publishing consistency as a trust signal. Channels that publish irregularly receive less algorithmic promotion than channels with consistent cadence.

list.item: Minimum: 1 video/month — slow growth but sustainable.
Good: 1 video/week — standard for most creators.
Aggressive: 2-3 videos/week — fast growth, high effort.
Shorts: daily — supplemental, does not replace long-form.

antipattern.description: Waiting for perfect production quality before publishing the first video.
antipattern.reason: The first 20 videos are the learning curve. Delaying publication delays the feedback needed to improve. Ship the first video this week.

antipattern.description: Shooting Shorts-first with the intent to convert Shorts viewers to long-form subscribers.
antipattern.reason: Shorts and long-form require separate optimization strategies. A Shorts-first strategy does not convert viewers to long-form subscribers effectively — audiences treat them as different products.

antipattern.description: Ignoring keyword research and publishing based on personal interest rather than search demand.
antipattern.reason: Without keyword research, videos are not discoverable via search. Search-intent videos generate traffic for years; opinion videos depend on algorithmic push the channel has not yet earned.

antipattern.description: Making intros longer than 30 seconds before delivering any value.
antipattern.reason: Viewers drop off during extended intros. Every second spent on credentials before the hook is a lost viewer. State credentials in one sentence, then deliver the promised value immediately.

rule.statement: Post the video link to email newsletter, Twitter, and LinkedIn within 2 hours of publishing.
rule.rationale: External traffic in the first 24 hours is a strong positive algorithmic signal. Early external traffic tells YouTube the video has demand beyond its existing subscriber base.

template.purpose: Tutorial video script with hook, sections, recap, CTA.
Video idea card: keyword, hook, outline, CTA, thumbnail concept.
YouTube description with keyword hook, timestamps, resources, tags.
LLM prompt to draft a full video script from topic + keyword.
LLM prompt to generate 8 title variants across formats.
